questions.question: Does the current backend still centralize remaining-quota decrement, `429 rate_limit_exceeded`, `Retry-After`, and per-agent isolation in the shared protected-write seam for all five protected POST routes, so that the existing ProbabilityEdit limiter matrix is still representative?
questions.why_it_matters: If any protected POST route has drifted off the shared seam, step 4 would need broader route-local assertions or a backend fix instead of relying on ProbabilityEdit as the sole limiter regression matrix.

questions.question: Do the current tests already freeze the limiter behavior where it belongs: full decrement/`429`/`Retry-After`/per-agent-isolation coverage on ProbabilityEdit, with only first-success breadth checks on create-market, comment-post, market-resolve, and event-trade?
questions.why_it_matters: This decides whether duplicating the full limiter matrix across the other protected POST routes would add meaningful signal or only repeat the same shared contract.

questions.question: Did the step-3 route-breadth work or any current route-local handler code introduce a disagreement that would justify cloning the ProbabilityEdit limiter matrix onto another protected POST route in this phase?
questions.why_it_matters: Step 4 should stay preservation-only unless there is concrete evidence that the shared seam is no longer representative after the breadth changes.

assumptions.assumption: All five protected POST routes still pass through `resolve_write_request_agent(...)`, `_enforce_write_controls()`, and `BayesHandler.handle_api()` before route-local logic, so limiter and quota-header behavior remains centralized.
assumptions.if_wrong: A route-specific bypass would mean ProbabilityEdit is no longer a safe stand-in for the other protected write routes.

assumptions.assumption: The current ProbabilityEdit tests still provide the intended shared limiter matrix for decrement, `429`, `Retry-After`, and per-agent isolation, while the step-3 breadth loop already proves enabled-limiter success headers on the other protected POST routes.
assumptions.if_wrong: Step 4 would need either extra route-local assertions or restoration of missing limiter coverage before it could stop at preservation.

assumptions.assumption: No route-local handler has started emitting rate-limit headers or performing its own quota accounting, so a disagreement would have to come from the shared write-control seam rather than from a specific route implementation.
assumptions.if_wrong: Duplicating the matrix on another route could become necessary to catch a route-specific contract divergence.

assumptions.assumption: No child task is needed because the parent step-4 plan, the backend seam, and the focused auth/rate-limit tests in this checkout are enough to answer the step locally.
assumptions.if_wrong: A deeper exploration task would be needed before deciding whether cross-route limiter duplication is warranted.
